article_metadata.title: Design and evaluation of effervescent floating tablets based on hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate using pentoxifylline as a model drug
article_metadata.overall_conclusion: Effervescent floating matrix tablets containing pentoxifylline with HEC and sodium alginate were successfully prepared; formulations with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate at 50–54 N hardness showed acceptable floating, swelling, and sustained release over 24 h in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2). [file:1]

sections_location.introduction: After abstract, describes gastroretentive systems and rationale for pentoxifylline and floating tablets. [file:1]

materials_and_methods.materials: Subsection 'Materials' lists origin of pentoxifylline, sodium alginate, sodium bicarbonate, Prosolv 90, hydroxyethyl cellulose (Natrosol 250-HHX), magnesium stearate. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.granulation_and_tablet_preparation: Subsections 'Granulation', 'Powder mixtures and granules evaluation', 'Tablets preparation', and 'Evaluation of tablets'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.FTIR_DSC: Subsections 'Differential scanning calorimetry study' and 'Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy'. [file:1]
materials_and_methods.in_vitro_tests: Subsections on 'Tablet floating capacity', 'Swelling and erosion studies', 'In vitro drug release studies', 'Release data modeling and analysis', and 'Statistical analysis'. [file:1]

sections_location.results_and_discussion: From 'Results and discussion' onward: moisture content, Carr’s index, DSC, FTIR, physical properties, floating, swelling, erosion, dissolution, and modeling. [file:1]
sections_location.conclusion: Final 'Conclusion' section summarizing optimal formulation and behavior. [file:1]

properties_mentioned.half_life: Approximately 1–2 h (short half-life) used as model highly water-soluble, high-density drug
properties_mentioned.dose_per_tablet: 60 mg per tablet for both 123_F1 and 123_F2

sodium_alginate.particle_size_used: 350 µm cut to improve compression compared with 180 µm cut

sodium_bicarbonate.role: Effervescent gas-forming agent to generate CO2 for floating

experimental_design_for_optimization.objective: To design and evaluate effervescent floating gastro-retentive matrix tablets with sustained release using mixtures of hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate, and to study the effects of granulation, sodium bicarbonate level, and tablet hardness. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: tablet_hardness
levels_N: 59–64 (Level C; attainable only from powder, not from granules for 123_F1/123_F2)

independent_variables.name: production_route
independent_variables.purpose: To compare flow, density, floating capacity, and release behavior before and after granulation.

responses_measured: Moisture content and Carr’s index (flow properties)
Tablet hardness, friability, weight uniformity, content uniformity
Floating lag time and total floating duration
Swelling and erosion (% medium uptake, % mass loss)
Dissolution profile (% drug released vs time in 0.1 N HCl, pH 1.2, 24 h)
Release kinetics (Korsmeyer–Peppas model parameters n and Kp)
Statistical significance via paired t-test and ANOVA

tests: post hoc multiple comparisons when needed

powder_preparation.sieving: All powders passed through 180 µm sieve; sodium alginate used at 350 µm cut to mitigate compression problems. [file:1]
powder_preparation.mixing: Turbula mixer at 60 rpm for 10 min for powder blends. [file:1]

wet_granulation.binder: 0.5% w/w water added to powder mixture
wet_granulation.equipment: Kenwood Chef/Kneader mixer, 10 min mixing, mass passed through 1000 µm sieve
wet_granulation.drying: Drying oven at 60 °C overnight; then granules collected at 853 µm size fraction

granule_evaluation.moisture_content: Measured using Mettler Toledo HG53 halogen moisture analyzer; values reported as mean ± SD for 123_F1 and 123_F2 pre- and post-granulation. [file:1]
granule_evaluation.flow_properties: Carr’s index calculated from bulk and tapped densities using tapping apparatus (Copley JV1000). [file:1]

UV_calibration.method: UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 274 nm in 0.1 N HCl for pentoxifylline; calibration curve prepared with triplicate measurements. [file:1]

123_F1.description: Floating matrix tablet with 10% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 10% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

123_F2.description: Floating matrix tablet with 20% w/w sodium bicarbonate.

composition_per_tablet_mg.magnesium_stearate: small amount to reach total tablet weight; difference due to lubricant content

approximate_fraction_of_tablet_mass.sodium_bicarbonate_percent: 20% w/w relative to matrix composition as stated by authors

nonfloating_control.description: Similar HEC–sodium alginate matrix tablets without sodium bicarbonate (0% w/w) used as control in swelling, erosion, and release modeling.
nonfloating_control.detailed_composition: Not fully tabulated in text; assumed to contain same polymer–filler–drug ratios as 123_F1/123_F2 without gas-former.

carr_index_F1.granules: Improved to fair flow (~17%; significant improvement, P < 0.05)

design_matrix.floating: Floating formulations when NaHCO3 used; 123_F1 tablets floated for 8–12 h depending on route and hardness.
123_F2 tablets floated for 24 h at both hardness levels for granule-based and powder-based tablets.
Nonfloating, used for comparison of swelling and erosion.

ftir_measurements.instrument: Perkin Elmer FT-IR system, Spectrum BX series
ftir_measurements.sample_presentation: Small quantity placed on sample stage and compressed by twisting arm; spectra acquired via Spectrum BX software. [file:1]

compatibility_results.F1_F2_powder_and_granules: Pentoxifylline characteristic peaks appeared at similar wavenumbers in mixtures and granules, indicating no chemical incompatibility with excipients.
compatibility_results.conclusion: No new peaks or major shifts indicative of strong interactions; formulation components are compatible.

equipment.tablet_press: Single-punch tableting machine Type 3, Manesty Machines Ltd, UK, with flat-faced 9.60 mm punches.
equipment.dissolution_apparatus: USP Apparatus II, Erweka GmbH, Germany.

consolidation_index_porosity.apparent_density: Increased with hardness and decreased after granulation, depending on formulation and NaHCO3 level. [file:1]
consolidation_index_porosity.porosity_percent: Decreased with higher hardness and with granulation; higher NaHCO3 reduced porosity further.

compression_process.machine: Single-punch Manesty Type 3, flat-faced 9.60 mm tools.
compression_process.process: Automatic compression from granules and manual compression from powder blends to achieve desired hardness levels.
compression_process.hardness_control: Adjusted via compression force to reach levels A–C; level C not achievable post-granulation for 123_F1/123_F2 due to elastic recovery of sodium alginate.

coating.status: No tablet coating reported; tablets are uncoated matrices.

environment_conditions.FTIR_conditions: Room temperature; spectra collected from 4000–620 cm−1.
environment_conditions.dissolution_temperature: 37 ± 0.5 °C in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2), paddle speed 50 rpm.

medium_and_conditions.sink_conditions: Maintained; solubility of pentoxifylline is high relative to dose.

F1_granule_tablets.total_floating_duration_h: ≈8 h (123_F1 granule tablets sank before 24 h).

level_A.mechanism: Anomalous (non-Fickian) transport with contributions from diffusion and polymer relaxation.
Anomalous (non-Fickian) with more pronounced polymer relaxation.

release_kinetics_summary.overall: Most granule-based formulations (floating and nonfloating) exhibit non-Fickian (anomalous) release as per the Korsmeyer–Peppas model, indicating combined diffusion and polymer relaxation/erosion. Increasing hardness tends to reduce Kp for powder-based tablets; granulation generally lowers Kp and increases n. [file:1]
release_kinetics_summary.zero_first_order: The article focuses on Korsmeyer–Peppas modeling rather than fitting detailed zero-order/first-order parameters; numeric zero/first-order constants are not tabulated.

swelling_water_uptake.method: Determined by medium uptake (DMU) at time intervals up to 24 h; tablets withdrawn, blotted, weighed wet, then dried at 60 °C to constant weight.

results_summary.trend_F2: Higher NaHCO3 (20% w/w) produced lower net swelling but higher mass loss due to more effervescence and longer floating, compared with nonfloating tablets and F1.
results_summary.trend_F1: 123_F1 granule tablets showed higher medium uptake near 24 h than 123_F2 when they had sunk and all surfaces were exposed.
results_summary.nonfloating: Nonfloating tablets showed highest swelling but lowest mass loss, as they remained fully submerged without effervescence.

tablet_mechanical_properties.hardness: All 123_F1/123_F2 granule tablets at levels A and B showed hardness around 5–6 kg with low variability (SD < 0.5 kg). [file:1]
tablet_mechanical_properties.friability: All <1% weight loss, meeting BP requirements; 123_F2 slightly higher friability at level A due to higher NaHCO3 content.
tablet_mechanical_properties.weight_variation: Twenty tablets tested for each formulation; deviations within BP limits.
tablet_mechanical_properties.thickness_and_diameter: Diameter fixed at 9.60 mm; thickness decreased slightly with increased hardness.

dissolution_profile.qualitative_F1: Granule-based 123_F1 tablets at level A released drug more slowly than powder-based 123_F1 at same hardness; increasing hardness reduced release rate. 123_F1 provided sustained release up to 24 h but with incomplete floating duration.
dissolution_profile.qualitative_F2: Granule-based 123_F2 at level A showed slower release than 123_F1 and nonfloating tablets due to longer floating and lower swelling. Increasing NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% decreased release rate for granule-based tablets but increased it for powder-based tablets (due to pore formation from gas).

stability.formal_stability_study: Not reported; study focuses on immediate post-manufacture performance, not long-term stability.

examples: Granulation significantly reduced moisture content and Carr’s index (P < 0.05) for both 123_F1 and F2.
Granulation significantly affected apparent density, porosity, floating lag time, and release profile in many cases (P < 0.05).
Raising NaHCO3 from 10 to 20% significantly changed floating lag time and drug release profiles for powder-based tablets; effect was attenuated after granulation.
Korsmeyer–Peppas fits showed R² values mostly >0.98, supporting the chosen model.
